Bipolar depressive episode with atypical features (hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis) is more common — similar to Type II.
DSM-5-TR — subtype abolished — as a specifier: ≥3 depressive symptoms during a manic episode or ≥3 manic symptoms during a depressive episode.
≥4 episodes within 12 months; poor prognostic marker.
6.1 Unified diagnostic criteria (DSM-5-TR · ICD-11 consensus points)
A. At least one manic episode
Manic and depressive episodes are not better explained by schizoaffective disorder, schizophrenia, or another psychotic disorder.
Depressive and hypomanic episodes are not required for Type I diagnosis, but are typically present
Mixed features specifier (DSM-IV mixed episode abolished); increased energy added as a criterion for manic episode (DSM-IV only mood).
Number of manic episodes and current episode type subqualifiers; rapid cycling as a qualifier.
diagnosis supported by structured interview and family information; antidepressant-triggered hypomanic/manic episode leads to bipolar diagnosis (in DSM-5-TR with criterion B qualifier).
Clinical interview + relative/friend information — manic and hypomanic episodes are more easily recognized by family members.

YMRS, HAM-D / MADRS, MDQ, HCL-32, C-SSRS.
Pre-lithium: complete blood count, creatinine, TSH, calcium, ECG, pregnancy test.
Pre-valproate: complete blood count, liver function, pregnancy test (for women of childbearing age — mandatory recommendation).
Before carbamazepine: complete blood count, liver, sodium.
8.1 General principles (NICE CG185 · CANMAT/ISBD 2018 · APA · WFSBP consensus)
Antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapine, aripiprazole, asenapine) ± mood stabilizer (lithium, valproate). Lorazepam adjunct in acute agitation.
quetiapine or lurasidone (FDA approval), olanzapine/fluoxetine combination (Symbyax); lithium or lamotrigine adjunctive therapy.
Manic switch and rapid cycling risk.
Lithium first-line — suicide reduction effect (Cipriani A. BMJ 2013); valproate, quetiapine, lamotrigine (especially with dominant depressive component), olanzapine alternatives.
Stop antidepressant; combination of lithium and valproate.
Psychoeducation, CBT for bipolar, IPSRT (Interpersonal and Social Rhythm Therapy — Frank E.), family-focused therapy (Miklowitz D.).
— Lithium: TSH, creatinine, calcium every 6 months; valproate: hepatic and hematologic.
valproate absolute contraindication (neural tube defects 4–6%, autism and IQ deficit); lamotrigine and quetiapine relatively safe.
Therapeutic serum level 0.6–1.0 mEq/L; in severe cases 0.8–1.2.
Manic, depressive, maintenance; reduces suicide risk.
Valproate (1000–2000 mg), carbamazepine (400–1200 mg), lamotrigine (200 mg titration)
Olanzapin, risperidon, kvetiapin, aripiprazol, asenapin, lurasidon, kariprazin
Manic, mixed, maintenance; quetiapine and lurasidone — FDA-approved for bipolar depression.
Caution in bipolar depressive episode; monotherapy.
CANMAT/ISBD 2018 (Yatham L.N. et al. Bipolar Disord 2018):
First-line for manic—lithium, valproate, quetiapine, aripiprazole, asenapine, paliperidone, risperidone; for bipolar depressive—quetiapine, lurasidone (monotherapy or adjunct with lithium/valproate).
Lithium is first-line maintenance; antidepressant alone without mood stabilizer.
Cipriani A. et al. BMJ 2013 meta-analiz:
Lithium significantly reduces suicide rates (superior to other mood stabilizers).
valproate in women of childbearing age — neural tube defects, autism, IQ decrease.
— Gold standard in bipolar disorder; reduces suicide; initial parameters creatinine, TSH, calcium, ECG; therapeutic level 0.6–1.0 mEq/L; toxicity > 1.5 mEq/L. Cipriani BMJ 2013.
Psychoeducation, early marker recognition, behavioral activation, compliance support. Lam D.H. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2003 RCT.
Interpersonal and Social Rhythm Therapy (IPSRT — Interpersonal and Social Rhythm Therapy) — Frank (Frank E.)
— Circadian rhythm and interpersonal stress management in bipolar disorder. Frank E. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2005 RCT — reduction of relapse in maintenance phase.
— Patient + family jointly; psychoeducation, communication, problem-solving. Miklowitz D.J. Am J Psychiatry 2003.
Paragraph 11. Manic episode severity: initial indicators and monitoring.
Mood Disorder Questionnaire (MDQ — Mood Disorder Questionnaire) — Hirshfeld (Hirschfeld R.M.)
In refractory manic or depressive episode, with mixed features, suicidal risk, a safe alternative in pregnancy. NICE TA59.
Episodic course; relapse 80–90% (untreated); significant reduction with maintenance pharmacotherapy.
Suicide risk high (6–7% lifetime); particularly in the first years.
Functional recovery moderate — the majority of patients experience significant social and occupational impairment.
Monitoring — mood scales, suicide risk, metabolic indicators, lithium level, compliance.
Myth 1: "Bipolar disorder is simple mood swings — everyone has them"

Bipolar manic episode requires both duration (≥7 days) and functional impairment; it is a distinct clinical condition from daily mood fluctuations. Manic episode may require psychotic features and hospitalization. Evidence: Merikangas Arch Gen Psychiatry 2011.
